Q1: I organize my study routine around hands-on practice rather than just reading. I typically block out time in the mornings when I feel most energetic, and I make sure I'm doing something active with the material—like working through problems or creating diagrams. With seven online courses, I've had to be strategic, so I prioritize based on what needs immediate attention. I keep a planner where I map out my week, though I'll admit my actual class attendance is pretty low because I find I learn better through self-directed practice.
Q2: My pattern definitely varies depending on what's due and what's happening in my life. I try to maintain some consistency, but honestly, when deadlines pile up, I shift into intensive mode and study in longer bursts. The stress of managing everything sometimes means my schedule gets chaotic, even though I know a regular routine would probably help me feel less overwhelmed. I've noticed I'm more productive when I stick to my morning blocks, but life doesn't always cooperate.
Q3: Usually I log in when I know there's something specific I need to do—check an assignment, watch a video that's been recommended, or see if there's new discussion happening. Sometimes I log in just to make sure I haven't missed anything important since my attendance is so low. The notifications on my phone also prompt me, which is helpful because otherwise I might forget amidst everything else. I'd say it's a mix of necessity and anxiety about falling behind.
Q4: Yes, checking the LMS became almost automatic, especially in the evenings after my extracurriculars. I'd sit down with my laptop and just naturally open it up to see what was new. I think it happened because I made it part of my wind-down routine—checking the platform while having tea. Once I started engaging more with discussion posts and seeing responses from peers, it felt more like a community I wanted to check in on rather than just a task.
Q5: The video content was incredibly useful for me—I watched over 270 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and really process things at my own pace. Discussion boards were also valuable because interacting with 41 peers helped me see different perspectives and solidify my understanding. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so anything I could actively engage with rather than passively read was gold for me. The combination of watching and then discussing made concepts stick.
Q6: I tended to ignore some of the supplementary reading materials, especially dense PDFs that felt like they were just walls of text. My learning style doesn't mesh well with sitting and reading for long periods—I need movement and interaction. I also delayed some of the self-assessment quizzes because they felt low-stakes and I prioritized the graded work. Looking back, I probably should have engaged with them more for practice, but time was always tight.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to either my exam performance or my actual understanding of the material. If I finished something and felt like I genuinely learned or could apply it, that was meaningful. I also valued activities where I could interact with others—the peer discussions energized me and made the learning feel less isolating. Anything that felt like busywork without clear purpose got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure but also less motivation to complete them. Required activities had deadlines and consequences, so they automatically got priority in my schedule. With optional ones, I'd think 'I'll get to this when I have time,' but that time rarely materialized. However, when I did engage with optional content, I often found it surprisingly valuable because I was choosing it out of genuine interest rather than obligation.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then work through them on my tablet while I was at the gym or commuting. I also used physical flashcards and practiced problems in a notebook—very hands-on stuff that wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. My textbooks got a lot of use too, especially when I needed to understand foundational concepts that the online materials glossed over.
Q10: There were many times I'd access a video once, download it, and then rewatch it multiple times offline while taking notes by hand. The LMS would only show one view, but I might have engaged with that content for hours. I also did a lot of collaborative studying with friends in person—we'd quiz each other, work through problems together, and explain concepts out loud. None of that peer learning showed up in my official interaction count.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd open a resource, save it to my device, and then close the LMS entirely. I preferred studying without the distraction of being online—notifications, the temptation to check other things. Once I had the materials downloaded, I could focus completely. My best study sessions were often completely offline, just me with printed notes and my highlighters, actively working through the material in a physical way.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially when my stress levels peaked. I'd feel this weight of everything I needed to do across all seven courses, and sometimes I'd just freeze. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me recognize when I was getting overwhelmed. During those periods, even logging into the LMS felt exhausting because it reminded me of everything pending. I had to actively work through those mental blocks.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor—with extracurriculars and this course load, I was often running on empty. When I was exhausted, I'd log in but just skim things rather than deeply engage. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly, and sometimes the LMS activities got deprioritized even when I knew they were important. The high stress definitely affected my ability to focus and retain information during those overwhelmed periods.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about other deadlines, personal stuff, or just feeling mentally checked out from exhaustion. I might watch a video but realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed anything. Those sessions felt unproductive, and I'd have to go back and redo them when I was in a better headspace.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered—my assignment completion wasn't perfect despite my high exam scores. Sometimes the anxiety of starting something big made me avoid it until the pressure became unbearable. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure, but honestly, it was partly fear of not doing well enough. The procrastination cycle was something I struggled with, especially when my resilience was low.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me—they created the urgency I needed to push through. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. I'd suddenly be logging in multiple times a day, watching videos, checking discussions for hints. Without deadlines, I'd drift and prioritize other things. It's not ideal, but that pressure was often what got me to perform at my best, even if it added to my stress.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my engagement—when I got positive feedback, I felt motivated to keep pushing and engage more deeply with similar content. When feedback was critical, I'd initially feel discouraged but then use it to focus my studying on weak areas. The timing mattered too; quick feedback kept me in the loop and adjusted my approach, while delayed feedback sometimes came too late to change my habits for that unit.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which was both good and bad. Good because I could fit studying around my extracurriculars and other courses. Bad because without forced attendance or rigid schedules, my low attendance became possible. The modular structure helped me chunk my learning into manageable pieces, but I sometimes lost the thread of how everything connected. Having clear milestones would have helped me stay more consistently engaged.
Q19: The video content being available on-demand made engagement easier—I could learn at my own pace and revisit confusing parts. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged real interaction, which I appreciated. What made it harder was when materials were only accessible online without download options, or when instructions were unclear and I had to figure things out alone. More interactive, hands-on activities would have suited my learning style better.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand but struggling with the material. They might watch videos repeatedly, read discussions, search for clarity—all that shows as high activity—but then feel too uncertain to submit work. It could also be perfectionism, where someone keeps engaging but never feels ready to commit to a final submission. Or they might be using the LMS to support studying for other related courses.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some degree. My exam scores were excellent despite not having the highest visible activity because so much of my real learning happened offline. I'd engage deeply with downloaded materials, practice problems on paper, and study with friends—none of which the LMS captured. Someone could be incredibly engaged and learning effectively through methods that simply don't generate digital footprints.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—when you're most active, which resources you access, how you interact with deadlines. They're good at capturing online behavior and identifying students who might be disengaging. But they miss the quality of engagement, the depth of understanding, and all the offline learning. They can't tell if someone is mindlessly clicking or deeply processing. They also miss the emotional and psychological dimensions of how someone is really doing.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively working with the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm asking questions, making connections, and genuinely curious about understanding deeper. For me, engagement is also about feeling connected—to the content, to my peers, to my own goals. It's a state where learning feels meaningful rather than just checking boxes. True engagement energizes me even when the work is challenging.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage my stress and stay focused. It wouldn't capture the surprise and excitement I felt when concepts clicked or how I celebrated my distinction with my family. It can't show the physical study methods I used—the flashcards spread across my floor, the diagrams I drew, the walks I took while listening to recorded lectures. My emotional journey through this course, the growth in managing my anxiety—that's invisible in the data.
